---
title: "Baltic Energy Transit Risk"
subtitle: "Exposure of post-2022 Baltic energy import infrastructure"
date: today
---

```{r stat-cards}
#| echo: false
#| output: asis
t  <- read.csv("data/processed/lng_terminals.csv", stringsAsFactors = FALSE)
op <- t[t$status == "operating", ]
n_term     <- nrow(t)
op_cap     <- round(sum(op$capacity_bcm), 1)
fsru_share <- round(100 * sum(op$capacity_bcm[op$type == "fsru"]) / sum(op$capacity_bcm))
cat(sprintf('
<div class="stat-row">
  <div class="stat-card"><div class="stat-num">%d</div><div class="stat-label">LNG import terminals mapped</div></div>
  <div class="stat-card"><div class="stat-num">%s</div><div class="stat-label">bcm / yr operating capacity</div></div>
  <div class="stat-card"><div class="stat-num">%d%%</div><div class="stat-label">of it on floating units (FSRUs)</div></div>
</div>', n_term, op_cap, fsru_share))
```

## The question

Before 2022, much of the Baltic region's gas arrived by pipeline from Russia.
After the invasion of Ukraine and the sabotage of the Nord Stream pipelines, that
supply was cut or abandoned, and imports moved onto a small set of coastal LNG
terminals and ports such as Klaipėda, Świnoujście, and Inkoo.

That shift concentrated a great deal of supply onto a few fixed points of
geography. This project asks how concentrated, and how exposed:

> After 2022, Baltic energy imports shifted onto a small set of coastal LNG
> terminals and ports. How concentrated is that infrastructure, and how exposed
> is it, measured by capacity, by distance to Russian territory, and by reliance
> on the single shipping route through the Danish Straits?

## How to read this site

- The [Data](01-data.qmd) page lists every source, all public, and says what is
  solid and what is still rough.
- The [Analysis](02-analysis.qmd) page holds the maps, the distance
  measurements, and the exposure ranking.

## Findings

The numbers behind these sit on the [Analysis](02-analysis.qmd) page. The
terminal figures are rough approximations (see the [Data](01-data.qmd) page), so
the rankings show the broad pattern of exposure, not precise measurement.

1. **Concentration.** Capacity is lopsided. Two southern terminals, Mukran in
   Germany and Świnoujście in Poland, hold most of the region's regasification
   capacity, while the northeast leans on one or two units each: Inkoo for the
   Gulf of Finland and Klaipėda for Lithuania.
2. **Proximity.** The terminals closest to Russian territory are the Gulf of
   Finland sites and Klaipėda, which sits near the Kaliningrad exclave. Proximity
   and capacity do not always line up, which is what the exposure score is built
   to show.
3. **Chokepoint dependence.** Every cargo bound for these terminals passes
   through the Danish Straits, so that single dependence is shared across the
   whole system. No terminal routes around it.

## What the exposure score is

The score combines three things, each given a stated weight:

- how close a terminal is to Russian territory,
- how large its share of regasification capacity is,
- how close it is to the Danish Straits.

The weights are written out on the Analysis page. The score is deliberately
simple. There is no hidden model, and anyone can change the weights and see how
the ranking moves.

## How the weights were chosen

The weights are a judgement, not a finding. Proximity to Russian territory gets
the most weight (0.4) because the question is about exposure to pressure from one
actor, and distance is the most direct measure the public data supports.
Capacity and chokepoint reliance each get 0.3: a larger terminal does more damage
if it goes down, and a terminal that can only be resupplied through the Danish
Straits shares one point of failure with every other cargo. None of this is
fixed. The three weights sit in plain sight on the Analysis page, and changing
them changes the ranking. The aim is a sensible starting point for discussion,
not a final score.

## Limitations

This measures geographic exposure, not the probability of disruption. A terminal
close to a maritime boundary is not a prediction that anything will happen to it.
It is a statement about geography.

A few specific caveats:

- The score uses proxy measures (distance, capacity share) rather than a causal
  model. Different reasonable weights would shift the rankings.
- It is a single snapshot. Capacity, routes, and terminals change.
- The terminal capacities are rough approximations to verify, not authoritative
  statistics. The dependence figures are Eurostat data for 2023. See the
  [Data](01-data.qmd) page.
- Undersea cable and pipeline incidents in the region between 2023 and 2025 are
  context for the question, not events modeled here.

In short, this shows where the exposure sits. It is not a risk forecast.
